summary: This post examines suspicious discontinuities across diverse domains - from tax policy to sports to drug prosecutions - where sharp thresholds create perverse incentives and non-smooth distributions. The author demonstrates how discontinuities lead to predictable gaming behavior: people losing money to qualify for health insurance subsidies, teachers inflating borderline test scores, prosecutors charging exactly at mandatory minimum thresholds, and birth month determining athletic careers. The common thread is that artificial bright-line rules create step functions in outcomes where smooth gradients would be more rational, and basic visualization techniques (histograms, scatterplots) reliably expose these patterns.
key_points: U.S. tax policy contains sharp income thresholds that can incentivize people to deliberately lose money to qualify for subsidies
Discontinuities appear across diverse domains: queues, college admissions, election fraud, drug charges, test scores, sports selection, and procurement auctions
Basic visualization tools (histograms, scatterplots, CDFs) are effective at detecting suspicious patterns caused by threshold effects
Randomization and gradual phase-outs are common solutions to smooth out problematic discontinuities
money_quotes: That means if an individual buying ACA insurance was going to earn $55k, they'd be better off reducing their income by $6440 and getting under the $48,560 subsidy ceiling than they are earning $55k.
A tax system that encourages people to lose money, perhaps by funneling it to (on average) much wealthier options traders by buying put options, seems sub-optimal.

Among people who don't qualify for a Pell Grant, it's those with the lowest income who are the most severely impacted and have the most severely reduced probability of admission. Among people who do qualify, it's those with the highest income who are mostly likely to benefit, again the opposite of what you'd probably want.

I've gotten a lot of mileage out in my career both out of being suspicious of discontinuities and figuring out where they come from and also out of applying standard techniques to smooth out discontinuities.
Basic tools like 'drawing a scatterplot', 'drawing a histogram', 'drawing the CDF' often come in handy.
key_insight: Bright-line thresholds in policy, systems, and measurement create predictable discontinuities that incentivize gaming and produce outcomes opposite to their intended purpose, but simple visualization techniques can reliably expose these patterns.
